hook: You already know how to summarize a single variable with means and standard deviations — but most real questions involve two variables at once, like how study time relates to test scores or whether taller people tend to earn more. So far, your tools can only describe one thing at a time, leaving the relationship between variables invisible. In this unit, you'll learn to see, measure, and model those relationships — then think critically about what they actually mean.
concept: The unifying idea is that two numerical variables often vary together in a predictable way, and we can capture that pattern with a straight line — a concept formally known as simple linear regression — to both describe and make predictions from real data.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to graph paired data on a scatter plot and describe the direction, form, and strength of the relationship between two variables.
Master the correlation coefficient $r$ as a precise numerical measure of how strongly and in what direction two quantitative variables are linearly associated.
You will learn to find and use a linear regression equation $\hat{y} = a + bx$ to model the relationship between two variables and make predictions.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Residuals and the Least Squares Line
topic_roadmap.description: Apply the concept of residuals — the differences between observed and predicted values — to understand why the least squares line is the best-fitting line for your data.
You will learn to interpret the slope $b$ and intercept $a$ of a regression line in the context of real data, expressing what each number actually means in plain language.
You will learn to distinguish between correlation and causation, recognizing why a strong statistical association between two variables does not prove that one causes the other.
